title: LLMs, Go Code!
topic: LLMs, Go Code! - The Developer's Guide to Designing, Prompting, and Auditing Go Systems

concepts.title: The Zen of Go: Why Boring Is a Superpower
Structs & Composition: Modeling Data Before the Agent Writes a Line
Implicit Interfaces: Building Sandboxes for Agent-Generated Code
Errors as Values: Catching an Agent That Cuts Corners
Goroutines, Channels & Context: Auditing What Agents Get Wrong Most
The Go Toolchain as an Automated Feedback Loop for Agents
Executable Specifications: Prompts, Interfaces & Schema-First Design
Test-Driven Agent Development: Red, Green, Refactor — with an LLM
Case Study: Building an API Service with a Human Architect

images.prompt: A clean, modern editorial illustration for a programming book cover: a stylized Go gopher silhouette (rounded body, two small ears, buck teeth suggestion) rendered as a friendly geometric mascot, sitting at a terminal, with a glowing blue/cyan circuit-like halo of code fragments and small robotic 'agent' cursor icons orbiting around it as if being directed by the gopher. Flat vector style, generous whitespace, warm off-white background, palette limited to Go's brand colors (gopher blue #00ADD8, aqua #5DC9E2, a touch of yellow #F2C400 and near-black ink), no readable text baked into the image. Target size ~1600x900px, 16:9 landscape; compose for a large cover hero with the subject centered and safe margins — it is letterboxed into a 16:9 box, so keep nothing important near the edges.
images.alt: A stylized Go gopher directing a halo of orbiting AI-agent code fragments
images.caption: The developer becomes the architect; the agent becomes the hands.

notes:
Requested look: light themed, with some colors from gopher icon here, https://go.dev/images/gophers/ladder.svg , side notes in yellow bordered boxes. (No matching preset theme id — treat as an inline art-direction brief: light background, Go-gopher-blue/cyan accents, warm yellow bordered callout boxes for side notes.)

Reference material: requester said to do our own internet research (no links supplied).

Requester's brainstorm / desired outline (use as a brief, not a rigid spec — choose ~6-12 concepts that best serve this audience, folding in the strongest ideas below):

The book's premise: the software engineer's role is shifting from manual syntax typing to system architecture, debugging, and orchestrating AI agents. Go is uniquely positioned for this shift: simple syntax, small language surface area, explicit error handling, and a strict compiler make it one of the easiest languages for AI agents to write correctly, yet one of the most critical for humans to structure and audit properly. Persona is 'the-crafter' (Crafting-Interpreters-style: calm, step-by-step, motivation -> design -> implementation -> edge cases -> reflection).

A 'Part 0: The Go Blueprint' idea — what a reader must know before agents write code, taught through the lens of agent-assisted development (how Go thinks teaches how to instruct agents):
- The Zen of Go: why boring/simple/explicit is a superpower for LLMs (fewer magic abstractions -> fewer hallucinations); keep agent output flat and explicit.
- Data modeling via composition over inheritance: structs/embedding; design clean structs first, then have agents write methods against them.
- Implicit interfaces as the ultimate decoupling tool: no 'implements' keyword, structural typing lets you define an interface and have an agent build a decoupled adapter package that satisfies it without tight coupling — a 'sandbox' for safe agent-generated code.
- Errors as values, not exceptions: agents get lazy with error handling (assigning to `_`, skipping checks) — the reader must recognize standard error patterns (errors.New, sentinel errors, custom error types, wrapping) to catch corner-cutting.
- Concurrency, goroutines, channels, and context.Context: where agents fail most (races, deadlocks, goroutine leaks) — the human must understand Go's memory/synchronization model to audit agent-generated concurrent code.
- The Go toolchain (go fmt, go test, go vet, go.mod) as an automated quality guard — wire it into an agentic feedback loop: compiler/linter failures get fed back to the agent to fix.

Other strong angles from the brainstorm to weave in:
- Writing 'executable specifications': prompts, context files, and Go interfaces that yield predictable agent output.
- Interface-driven design and modular-monolith package structure so agents don't hallucinate cross-package dependencies; schema-first design (OpenAPI/gRPC/Protobuf/SQL) as a source of truth agents generate boilerplate from.
- Auditing AI-generated Go: common hallucinations (unclosed response bodies / missing defer resp.Body.Close(), improper error wrapping, ignored errors), and using go test -race, go vet, and pprof to evaluate agent output quality.
- Test-Driven Agent Development: writing precise table-driven tests first so an agent has clear inputs/outputs/edge cases to implement against; mocking interfaces so agents can build isolated components; a red-green-refactor loop with agents.
- A worked example building an API service (schema/routes designed by the human, handlers/DB logic generated by an agent) and a CLI tool (prompting for flag parsing and OS interactions).

The throughline for every chapter: teach the Go concept properly (this is still a real Go book), then add 'the agent angle' — why this feature matters specifically when a human is directing/reviewing/prompting an LLM coding agent, and a concrete 'key takeaway' / audit checklist the reader can apply to agent output.
